title: Indonesia Beats The Count, For Now
text:
Indonesia Beats The Count, For Now

Tom Miller, Udith Sikand

Gavekal Research | 22 Jun 2026

As the energy shock fades, emerging Asia’s battered asset markets are climbing off the canvas. Not least Indonesia, which after being hit hard by the oil crisis appears to have avoided the knockout blow of losing its MSCI “emerging market” status. Staying in the EM index will keep Indonesian stocks in global investment mandates, preventing a further wave of foreign capital outflows and providing a much-needed confidence boost. But the recovery could prove short-lived if policymakers do not change tack at home.The first reason to be optimistic is that, if the US-Iran deal holds, the cost of fuel subsidies will fall dramatically. The government budget for 2026 initially penciled in energy subsidies worth IDR210trn (US$11.8bn), about 1% of GDP, based on a crude oil price of US$70/bbl. But shortly after the Iran war started, the government expanded its subsidies budget by IDR100trn, sparking fears about Indonesia’s deteriorating fiscal position.
